question:
9. Find all the solutions of the equation
\[
y y^{\prime \prime}-2\left(y^{\prime}\right)^{2}=0
\]
which pass through the point \( x=1, y=1 \).
solution:
First Solution. \( 1 / y^{3} \) is an integrating factor since
\[
\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{y^{\prime}}{y^{2}}\right)=\frac{y y^{\prime \prime}-2\left(y^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{y^{3}}=0 .
\]

Therefore \( y^{\prime} / y^{2}=C \) and \( -1 / y=C x+D \) for appropriate constants \( C \) and \( D \). In order that the solution pass through ( 1,1 ), we require that \( C+D=-1 \). Hence
\[
y=\frac{1}{1+C(1-x)} .
\]

Conversely, any function of this form satisfies the equation and the initial conditions. If \( C=0 \), this is a constant function and its domain may be taken as \( (-\infty,+\infty) \). If \( C \neq 0 \), the right member of (1) becomes infinite for \( x=(1+C) / C \), so the domain of (1) must be restricted to
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\left(-\infty, \frac{1+C}{C}\right) \text { if } C>0 \\
\left(\frac{1+C}{C}, \infty\right) \text { if } C<0
\end{array}
\]

Second Solution. Since \( x \) does not appear explicitly in the given differential equation, the substitution \( v=y^{\prime}, y^{\prime \prime}=v d v / d y \) leads to a firstorder equation
\[
v\left(y \frac{d v}{d y}-2 v\right)=0
\]

Hence either \( v=0 \) and \( y \) is constant, or
\[
y \frac{d v}{d y}-2 v=0
\]

In this case the variables are separable and we obtain
\[
v=C y^{2},
\]
that is,
\[
y^{\prime}=C y^{2},
\]
which is again separable. We get
\[
-1 / y=C x+D
\]
and the solution proceeds as before.
Note that the special solution \( y= \) constant is subsumed in this general case.
If the original equation is solved for the highest derivative
\[
y^{\prime \prime}=\frac{2\left(y^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{y}
\]
it becomes clear that this differential equation is regular in the upper and lower half-planes but may be singular along the \( x \)-axis. It is obvious that all solutions (1) are maximal solutions, since they cannot be extended continuously to any larger connected domain. Since none of these solutions passes through any point where the equation might be singular, we are assured that we have found all of the solutions passing through \( (1,1) \).

descriptive_long.question:
9. Find all the solutions of the equation
\[
ordinate \, ordinate^{\prime \prime}-2\left(ordinate^{\prime}\right)^{2}=0
\]
which pass through the point \( abscissa=1, ordinate=1 \).
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. \( 1 / ordinate^{3} \) is an integrating factor since
\[
\frac{d}{d abscissa}\left(\frac{ordinate^{\prime}}{ordinate^{2}}\right)=\frac{ordinate \, ordinate^{\prime \prime}-2\left(ordinate^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{ordinate^{3}}=0 .
\]

Therefore \( ordinate^{\prime} / ordinate^{2}=scalefactor \) and \( -1 / ordinate=scalefactor \, abscissa+shiftparam \) for appropriate constants \( scalefactor \) and \( shiftparam \). In order that the solution pass through ( 1,1 ), we require that \( scalefactor+shiftparam=-1 \). Hence
\[
ordinate=\frac{1}{1+scalefactor(1-abscissa)} .
\]

Conversely, any function of this form satisfies the equation and the initial conditions. If \( scalefactor=0 \), this is a constant function and its domain may be taken as \( (-\infty,+\infty) \). If \( scalefactor \neq 0 \), the right member of (1) becomes infinite for \( abscissa=(1+scalefactor) / scalefactor \), so the domain of (1) must be restricted to
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\left(-\infty, \frac{1+scalefactor}{scalefactor}\right) \text { if } scalefactor>0 \\
\left(\frac{1+scalefactor}{scalefactor}, \infty\right) \text { if } scalefactor<0
\end{array}
\]

Second Solution. Since \( abscissa \) does not appear explicitly in the given differential equation, the substitution \( velocity=ordinate^{\prime},\; ordinate^{\prime \prime}=velocity \, d velocity / d ordinate \) leads to a first-order equation
\[
velocity\left(ordinate \frac{d velocity}{d ordinate}-2 \, velocity\right)=0
\]

Hence either \( velocity=0 \) and \( ordinate \) is constant, or
\[
ordinate \frac{d velocity}{d ordinate}-2 \, velocity=0
\]

In this case the variables are separable and we obtain
\[
velocity=scalefactor \, ordinate^{2},
\]
that is,
\[
ordinate^{\prime}=scalefactor \, ordinate^{2},
\]
which is again separable. We get
\[
-1 / ordinate=scalefactor \, abscissa+shiftparam
\]
and the solution proceeds as before.
Note that the special solution \( ordinate= \) constant is subsumed in this general case.
If the original equation is solved for the highest derivative
\[
ordinate^{\prime \prime}=\frac{2\left(ordinate^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{ordinate}
\]
it becomes clear that this differential equation is regular in the upper and lower half-planes but may be singular along the \( abscissa \)-axis. It is obvious that all solutions (1) are maximal solutions, since they cannot be extended continuously to any larger connected domain. Since none of these solutions passes through any point where the equation might be singular, we are assured that we have found all of the solutions passing through \( (1,1) \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
9. Find all the solutions of the equation
\[
screwdriver screwdriver^{\prime \prime}-2\left(screwdriver^{\prime}\right)^{2}=0
\]
which pass through the point \( pineapple=1, screwdriver=1 \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. \( 1 / screwdriver^{3} \) is an integrating factor since
\[
\frac{d}{d pineapple}\left(\frac{screwdriver^{\prime}}{screwdriver^{2}}\right)=\frac{screwdriver screwdriver^{\prime \prime}-2\left(screwdriver^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{screwdriver^{3}}=0 .
\]

Therefore \( screwdriver^{\prime} / screwdriver^{2}=lawnmower \) and \( -1 / screwdriver=lawnmower pineapple+paperclips \) for appropriate constants lawnmower and paperclips. In order that the solution pass through ( 1,1 ), we require that \( lawnmower+paperclips=-1 \). Hence
\[
screwdriver=\frac{1}{1+lawnmower(1-pineapple)} .
\]

Conversely, any function of this form satisfies the equation and the initial conditions. If \( lawnmower=0 \), this is a constant function and its domain may be taken as \( (-\infty,+\infty) \). If \( lawnmower \neq 0 \), the right member of (1) becomes infinite for \( pineapple=(1+lawnmower) / lawnmower \), so the domain of (1) must be restricted to
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\left(-\infty, \frac{1+lawnmower}{lawnmower}\right) \text { if } lawnmower>0 \\
\left(\frac{1+lawnmower}{lawnmower}, \infty\right) \text { if } lawnmower<0
\end{array}
\]

Second Solution. Since \( pineapple \) does not appear explicitly in the given differential equation, the substitution \( tangerine=screwdriver^{\prime}, screwdriver^{\prime \prime}=tangerine d tangerine / d screwdriver \) leads to a firstorder equation
\[
tangerine\left(screwdriver \frac{d tangerine}{d screwdriver}-2 tangerine\right)=0
\]

Hence either \( tangerine=0 \) and \( screwdriver \) is constant, or
\[
screwdriver \frac{d tangerine}{d screwdriver}-2 tangerine=0
\]

In this case the variables are separable and we obtain
\[
tangerine=lawnmower screwdriver^{2},
\]
that is,
\[
screwdriver^{\prime}=lawnmower screwdriver^{2},
\]
which is again separable. We get
\[
-1 / screwdriver=lawnmower pineapple+paperclips
\]
and the solution proceeds as before.
Note that the special solution \( screwdriver= \) constant is subsumed in this general case.
If the original equation is solved for the highest derivative
\[
screwdriver^{\prime \prime}=\frac{2\left(screwdriver^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{screwdriver}
\]
it becomes clear that this differential equation is regular in the upper and lower half-planes but may be singular along the \( pineapple \)-axis. It is obvious that all solutions (1) are maximal solutions, since they cannot be extended continuously to any larger connected domain. Since none of these solutions passes through any point where the equation might be singular, we are assured that we have found all of the solutions passing through \( (1,1) \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
9. Find all the solutions of the equation
\[
independentval independentval^{\prime \prime}-2\left(independentval^{\prime}\right)^{2}=0
\]
which pass through the point \( immutabledatum=1, independentval=1 \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. \( 1 / independentval^{3} \) is an integrating factor since
\[
\frac{d}{d immutabledatum}\left(\frac{independentval^{\prime}}{independentval^{2}}\right)=\frac{independentval independentval^{\prime \prime}-2\left(independentval^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{independentval^{3}}=0 .
\]

Therefore \( independentval^{\prime} / independentval^{2}=varyingentity \) and \( -1 / independentval=varyingentity immutabledatum+mutablefactor \) for appropriate constants \( varyingentity \) and \( mutablefactor \). In order that the solution pass through ( 1,1 ), we require that \( varyingentity+mutablefactor=-1 \). Hence
\[
independentval=\frac{1}{1+varyingentity(1-immutabledatum)} .
\]

Conversely, any function of this form satisfies the equation and the initial conditions. If \( varyingentity=0 \), this is a constant function and its domain may be taken as \( (-\infty,+\infty) \). If \( varyingentity \neq 0 \), the right member of (1) becomes infinite for \( immutabledatum=(1+varyingentity) / varyingentity \), so the domain of (1) must be restricted to
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\left(-\infty, \frac{1+varyingentity}{varyingentity}\right) \text { if } varyingentity>0 \\
\left(\frac{1+varyingentity}{varyingentity}, \infty\right) \text { if } varyingentity<0
\end{array}
\]

Second Solution. Since \( immutabledatum \) does not appear explicitly in the given differential equation, the substitution \( stillnessfactor=independentval^{\prime}, independentval^{\prime \prime}=stillnessfactor d stillnessfactor / d independentval \) leads to a firstorder equation
\[
stillnessfactor\left(independentval \frac{d stillnessfactor}{d independentval}-2 stillnessfactor\right)=0
\]

Hence either \( stillnessfactor=0 \) and \( independentval \) is constant, or
\[
independentval \frac{d stillnessfactor}{d independentval}-2 stillnessfactor=0
\]

In this case the variables are separable and we obtain
\[
stillnessfactor=varyingentity independentval^{2},
\]
that is,
\[
independentval^{\prime}=varyingentity independentval^{2},
\]
which is again separable. We get
\[
-1 / independentval=varyingentity immutabledatum+mutablefactor
\]
and the solution proceeds as before.
Note that the special solution \( independentval= \) constant is subsumed in this general case.
If the original equation is solved for the highest derivative
\[
independentval^{\prime \prime}=\frac{2\left(independentval^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{independentval}
\]
it becomes clear that this differential equation is regular in the upper and lower half-planes but may be singular along the \( immutabledatum \)-axis. It is obvious that all solutions (1) are maximal solutions, since they cannot be extended continuously to any larger connected domain. Since none of these solutions passes through any point where the equation might be singular, we are assured that we have found all of the solutions passing through \( (1,1) \).

garbled_string.question:
Find all the solutions of the equation
\[
plomtrqz \, plomtrqz^{\prime \prime}-2\left(plomtrqz^{\prime}\right)^{2}=0
\]
which pass through the point \( qzxwvtnp=1, plomtrqz=1 \).
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. \( 1 / plomtrqz^{3} \) is an integrating factor since
\[
\frac{d}{d qzxwvtnp}\left(\frac{plomtrqz^{\prime}}{plomtrqz^{2}}\right)=\frac{plomtrqz \, plomtrqz^{\prime \prime}-2\left(plomtrqz^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{plomtrqz^{3}}=0 .
\]

Therefore \( plomtrqz^{\prime} / plomtrqz^{2}=frabdexi \) and \( -1 / plomtrqz=frabdexi \, qzxwvtnp+dromsple \) for appropriate constants \( frabdexi \) and \( dromsple \). In order that the solution pass through ( 1,1 ), we require that \( frabdexi+dromsple=-1 \). Hence
\[
plomtrqz=\frac{1}{1+frabdexi(1-qzxwvtnp)} .
\]

Conversely, any function of this form satisfies the equation and the initial conditions. If \( frabdexi=0 \), this is a constant function and its domain may be taken as \( (-\infty,+\infty) \). If \( frabdexi \neq 0 \), the right member of (1) becomes infinite for \( qzxwvtnp=(1+frabdexi) / frabdexi \), so the domain of (1) must be restricted to
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\left(-\infty, \frac{1+frabdexi}{frabdexi}\right) \text { if } frabdexi>0 \\
\left(\frac{1+frabdexi}{frabdexi}, \infty\right) \text { if } frabdexi<0
\end{array}
\]

Second Solution. Since \( qzxwvtnp \) does not appear explicitly in the given differential equation, the substitution \( snaglyru=plomtrqz^{\prime},\, plomtrqz^{\prime \prime}=snaglyru \, d snaglyru / d plomtrqz \) leads to a firstorder equation
\[
snaglyru\left(plomtrqz \frac{d snaglyru}{d plomtrqz}-2 snaglyru\right)=0
\]

Hence either \( snaglyru=0 \) and \( plomtrqz \) is constant, or
\[
plomtrqz \frac{d snaglyru}{d plomtrqz}-2 snaglyru=0
\]

In this case the variables are separable and we obtain
\[
snaglyru=frabdexi \, plomtrqz^{2},
\]
that is,
\[
plomtrqz^{\prime}=frabdexi \, plomtrqz^{2},
\]
which is again separable. We get
\[
-1 / plomtrqz=frabdexi \, qzxwvtnp+dromsple
\]
and the solution proceeds as before.
Note that the special solution \( plomtrqz= \) constant is subsumed in this general case.
If the original equation is solved for the highest derivative
\[
plomtrqz^{\prime \prime}=\frac{2\left(plomtrqz^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{plomtrqz}
\]
it becomes clear that this differential equation is regular in the upper and lower half-planes but may be singular along the \( qzxwvtnp \)-axis. It is obvious that all solutions (1) are maximal solutions, since they cannot be extended continuously to any larger connected domain. Since none of these solutions passes through any point where the equation might be singular, we are assured that we have found all of the solutions passing through \( (1,1) \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let \Omega  be a connected open neighbourhood of the origin in \mathbb{R}^3 and let  
u : \Omega  \to  (0,\infty ) be a twice-continuously-differentiable function that fulfils the
coupled nonlinear system  

(E1) u \Delta u - 4 |\nabla u|^2 = 0,  

(E2) |\nabla u|^2 = \kappa  u^8  for some (a-priori unknown) positive constant \kappa ,  

where \Delta  and \nabla  denote, respectively, the three-dimensional Laplacian and gradient.
Assume the Cauchy data  

u(0,0,0)=2,  \nabla u(0,0,0)= (a,0,0) with a \neq  0.  

(a) Determine explicitly every function u that satisfies (E1)-(E2) together with
the above initial data, and express \kappa  in terms of a.  

(b) For each solution, describe the maximal open set \Omega _max on which it can be
extended smoothly.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write x=(x,y,z) and e_1=(1,0,0).

Step 1.  A linearising substitution for (E1).  
Set  

 v := u^{-3}.  

A direct computation gives  

 \nabla v = -3u^{-4}\nabla u,  \Delta v = 12u^{-5}|\nabla u|^2 - 3u^{-4}\Delta u.

Hence

 u^5\Delta v = 12|\nabla u|^2 - 3u\Delta u
    = 12|\nabla u|^2 - 3\cdot 4|\nabla u|^2 = 0,

and because u>0 we conclude

  \Delta v = 0;  v is harmonic on \Omega .           (1)

Step 2.  Consequence of the additional constraint (E2).  
Condition (E2) together with \nabla v = -3u^{-4}\nabla u implies  

 |\nabla v|^2 = 9u^{-8}|\nabla u|^2 = 9\kappa  =: L^2,        (2)

i.e. the gradient of v has constant Euclidean length L=3\sqrt{\kappa}  independent of the
point x\in \Omega .

Step 3.  Classification of harmonic functions with constant gradient length.  
For a C^2 scalar field w the Bochner formula reads

  \Delta |\nabla w|^2 = 2 |D^2w|^2,        (3)

where D^2w is the Hessian of w and |D^2w|^2 the sum of the squares of its entries.
Applying (3) to the harmonic function v and using (2), the left-hand side of
(3) vanishes; therefore

  |D^2v|^2 = 0 \Rightarrow  D^2v \equiv  0 on \Omega .  

Hence v is an affine harmonic polynomial of degree 1, i.e. there exist a
constant vector p\in \mathbb{R}^3 and a constant d\in \mathbb{R} such that  

  v(x)=p\cdot x+d  for every x\in \Omega .        (4)

Because |p| = |\nabla v| = L, relation (2) supplies the additional information  

  |p| = 3\sqrt{\kappa} .           (5)

Step 4.  Imposing the Cauchy data.  
Since u(0)=2, we have v(0)=1/2^3=1/8, so d=1/8.  
Moreover,

 p = \nabla v(0) = -3u(0)^{-4}\nabla u(0) = -3\cdot 2^{-4}(a,0,0) = -(3a/16) e_1.  (6)

Thus

  v(x,y,z)= -(3a/16)x + 1/8.        (7)

Taking norms in (6) and comparing with (5) yields the numerical value of \kappa :

 |p| = 3|a|/16 = 3\sqrt{\kappa}  \Rightarrow  \kappa  = a^2/256.    (8)

Step 5.  Recovering u.  
Inverting v=u^{-3} gives

 u(x,y,z) = [1/8 - (3a/16)x]^{-1/3}
     = 2\cdot [1 - (3a/2)x]^{-1/3}.     (9)

Step 6.  Maximal domain of smoothness.  
The bracket in (9) must stay positive.  Writing

 x_0 := 2/(3a),

we obtain  

 \Omega _max = { (x,y,z) \in  \mathbb{R}^3  |  x < x_0 } if a>0,  
 \Omega _max = { (x,y,z) \in  \mathbb{R}^3  |  x > x_0 } if a<0.  (10)

Along the plane x=x_0 the denominator vanishes and u blows up to +\infty , so the
half-space (10) is the maximal connected open set on which u can be extended
smoothly.

Step 7.  Verification and uniqueness.  
A straightforward differentiation of (9) shows that

 |\nabla u|^2 = (a^2/256) u^8 and u\Delta u - 4|\nabla u|^2 = 0

everywhere on \Omega _max, so (E1)-(E2) and the initial data are satisfied.
Conversely, any solution of (E1)-(E2) gives rise, via v=u^{-3}, to a harmonic
function v with constant |\nabla v|; Step 3 forces such a v to be affine, and
Step 4 then fixes it uniquely.  Hence (9) is the only possible solution.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Dimension jump:  the original one–dimensional ODE is replaced by a
three–dimensional system (a nonlinear PDE plus a first–order vector
constraint).  
2. Two coupled equations:  solving (E1) alone still leaves huge freedom;
(E2) couples all first derivatives and forces an additional potential
structure.  
3. Transformation to harmonic functions in higher dimensions requires
facility with vector calculus identities, the maximum principle and
Liouville’s theorem, none of which appear in the original problem.  
4. The solution demands identification of an affine harmonic function via
exploiting both PDE and curl‐condition, an argument that uses
divergence–curl tools rather than elementary separation of variables.  
5. The maximal domain must be deduced from the blow–up set of an affine
denominator in ℝ³, not merely from a pole on the real line; the answer
depends on the sign of the given constant a.  

All these features make the enhanced variant substantially harder than both
the original and the kernel version: the solver must blend techniques from
PDE theory, harmonic analysis, vector calculus, and the theory of analytic
initial value problems, going well beyond the separable‐ODE tricks that
solve the original exercise.
